text: Robotically-assisted laparoscopic single-site sacrococopexy for cystocele and rectocele. Cystocele and rectocele are common findings in women after childbearing and often occur concomitantly. In a sacrococopexy, the cervix is dissected free from the bladder anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly. Subsequently, a graft made of permanent synthetic mesh is attached to the anterior and posterior aspects of the cervix at one end and the sacrum at the other. Our patient is a forty-five-year-old multiparous female presenting with a Grade II Cystocele and Rectocele with a history of a robotically assisted laparoscopic superservical hysterectomy. One year prior, she had failed a traditional anterior repair.

In order to optimize access to the vagina, side docking of the da Vinci SI robotic platform is employed after the patient is placed in dorsal lithotomy positioning with Allen boots. Intraperitoneal access is achieved using the open technique, placing the robotic single incision port through the umbilicus. An eight millimeter assisted port was placed in the right upper quadrant, lateral to the rectus muscle and slightly cephalad to the level of the umbilicus. The assistant port allows the placement and utilization of wristed robotic instruments, which is currently still a limitation of single-sided instruments. The pelvis is surveyed after the patient is placed in Trendelenburg positioning and pneumoperitoneum is achieved. The sacral promontory is identified and the ureter is visualized. The bladder is retrograde instilled through the Foley catheter to delineate its borders.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum overlying the cervix using monopolar shears, revealing the pubic cervical fascia as the peritoneum is dissected away. Adhesiolysis is performed anteriorly to the cervix using sharp dissection and electrosurgical energy, aided by a medium malleable retractor in the vagina. Posteriorly, the peritoneum is tented and dissected off the cervix, again exposing the pubic cervical fascia. The completed anterior and posterior dissections are visible before mesh placement. Extracorporeally, the polypropylene Y mesh is prepared, with an arm sutured in an accordion fashion for intra-abdominal management. The mesh is placed over the cervical stump, and its apex is secured to the cervix with permanent interrupted sutures.

The surgeon secures the posterior aspect of the cervix to the mesh using permanent sutures. The posterior vaginal component is sutured to the mesh with vicryl suture. The anterior arm of the mesh is released from its sutures and secured over the anterior aspect of the cervix.

The surgeon secures the mesh under traction, identifying the sacral promontory and incising the overlying peritoneum. The incision is extended medially to the posterior vaginal incision to prevent ureteric injury. The thin anterior longitudinal ligament and middle sacral vessels are dissected, with the vessels desiccated using fenestrated bipolar forceps to prevent hemorrhage. The mesh is attached to the anterior longitudinal ligament at the sacrum's hollow using permanent interrupted sutures. The base of the mesh is shortened for anatomical fit, and the final mesh placement from the cervix to the sacral promontory is shown. The mesh is re-peritonealized with a two-zero V-lock suture to prevent bowel entrapment.

Cystoscopy was subsequently performed, noting patency of the bilateral ureteral jets. The patient was discharged on postoperative day zero after a voiding trial. Postoperatively, the vaginal apex was noted to be well supported, and the patient is voiding and defecating without difficulty.
